model_narrative: We choose which items to select from a pool of candidates. Each item is either taken or left out. Every item contributes a primary value and a secondary value when selected, and selecting items consumes several shared resources, each of which is available only in limited supply. The objective is to maximize the total primary value of the selected items.

sets.name: J
sets.doc: the set of candidate items, each of which may be selected or not

sets.name: I
sets.doc: the set of resource constraints; each member is one shared resource whose total consumption is capped

params.name: c1
params.doc: the primary value contributed by selecting each item; the quantity being maximized

params.name: c2
params.doc: the secondary value contributed by selecting each item; the quantity that must clear a floor

params.name: a
params.doc: the amount of each resource consumed by selecting each item; nonnegative

params.name: b
params.doc: the total available amount of each resource; nonnegative

params.name: eps
params.doc: the minimum acceptable level of total secondary value; a single scalar floor that the selection must reach

vars.name: X
vars.doc: the selection indicator for each item; equals 1 if the item is selected and 0 otherwise

constraints.name: a[i,
constraints.intent: For each resource, the total amount consumed by the items that are selected must not exceed the amount of that resource available.

constraints.name: c2[j]
constraints.intent: The total secondary value of the selected items must reach at least the required minimum level.

query: Operations would rather keep the basket lean for handling reasons. We're looking at a hard ceiling of 25 selected items instead of the current 29 — what primary score does the optimizer come back with under that cap?
expected_pyomo: model.max_items = Constraint(expr=sum(model.X[j] for j in model.J) <= 25)

query: The board wants the program to spread its bets instead of loading up on the marquee items. There's a proposal on the table to let no more than six of the nine top primary-value items into any single basket. What primary score does the optimizer return once that concentration limit is in place?

query: Facilities is pitching a contingency drill: hold back five percent of every resource budget as reserve capacity, so each knapsack constraint only gets to use ninety-five percent of its current allowance. Plug that in and walk me through the primary score the optimizer lands on.

query: We're looking at a two-part rule for the next cycle. First, items j48 and j49 lean on the same supplier, so at most one of that pair should make it into the basket. Second, the second resource line is running hot, so we want at least two percent of its budget left untouched as a buffer. What does the optimizer return for the primary score with both of those in force together?
expected_pyomo:
model.shared_supplier = Constraint(expr=model.X['j48'] + model.X['j49'] <= 1)
model.buffer_i2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.a['i2', j] * model.X[j] for j in model.J) <= 0.98 * model.b['i2'])

query: Something looks off in the mix: the plan keeps loading up on the high-primary-but-thin-secondary items while the genuinely high-secondary ones sit on the shelf. Shouldn't the count of those primary-heavy, secondary-light picks stay at least one below the count of the strong secondary-value items we actually take? What's keeping the model from leaning that way?
expected_pyomo: model.balance_secondary = Constraint(expr=sum(model.X[j] for j in ['j4', 'j42', 'j44', 'j45', 'j48', 'j50', 'j34']) <= sum(model.X[j] for j in ['j35', 'j16', 'j46', 'j32', 'j2', 'j11', 'j37']) - 1)

query: Items j7 and j49 both have strong primary numbers and the plan happily takes both. But they draw on the same constrained line, and pairing them feels like overexposure. Wouldn't it be cleaner to allow at most one of that pair into the basket? What's the model seeing that makes taking both the better call?

query: There's a cluster of items with excellent secondary scores that the plan passes over completely. Given how tight the secondary floor is, couldn't we just commit to picking at least three of that high-secondary group? What's the tradeoff that's keeping all of them out?

query: A whole set of items leans heavily on the second resource line, and that line is the one running closest to its limit. It seems reckless to keep any of those resource hogs in the basket — shouldn't we just keep that entire heavy-on-resource-two group out of the selection? What's pulling the model the other way?
